paper.title: Video Laryngoscopy Versus Direct Laryngoscopy for Critically Ill Adults: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
paper.abstract: Objective: To compare first-pass success and hypoxemia with video versus direct laryngoscopy among critically ill adults. Data Sources: MEDLINE, Embase, and trial registries through May 2026. Study Selection: Randomized trials and prospective cohorts in emergency departments and ICUs. Data Synthesis: Twenty-one studies including 9340 intubations were included. Video laryngoscopy increased first-pass success (OR 1.42, 95% CI 1.18 to 1.71) and reduced severe hypoxemia (OR 0.78, 95% CI 0.63 to 0.97). Conclusions: Video laryngoscopy improves airway outcomes in critically ill adults.

reasons: topic match: emergency department, emergency medicine, critical care, ICU
journal boost: Annals of Emergency Medicine

paper.title: Early Vasopressor Strategy for Septic Shock in the Emergency Department: A Multicenter Randomized Trial
paper.abstract: Background: Early septic shock management in the emergency department may influence mortality. Methods: We randomized 1280 adult patients with septic shock to early norepinephrine plus usual care or usual care alone. The primary outcome was 28-day mortality. Results: Mortality was 21.4% in the early norepinephrine group and 25.8% in usual care (risk ratio 0.83, 95% CI 0.70 to 0.99). Serious adverse events were similar. Conclusions: Early norepinephrine reduced 28-day mortality without increasing serious adverse events.

reasons: topic match: emergency department, critical care, septic shock, vasopressor

paper.title: Balanced Crystalloids in Adults With Traumatic Hemorrhagic Shock: A Pragmatic Cluster Trial
paper.abstract: Importance: Fluid selection for traumatic hemorrhagic shock remains uncertain. Methods: In a pragmatic cluster randomized trial, 44 emergency departments assigned 5162 adults with suspected hemorrhagic shock to balanced crystalloids or saline during initial resuscitation. The primary outcome was 30-day mortality. Results: Mortality was 18.9% with balanced crystalloids and 21.2% with saline (adjusted OR 0.87, 95% CI 0.76 to 1.00). Acute kidney injury was lower with balanced crystalloids. Conclusions: Balanced crystalloids may modestly reduce mortality and kidney injury.

reasons: topic match: emergency department, resuscitation, trauma
